user_prompt: My Codex login has keys stored here "C:\Users\frank\.codex\auth.json", or otherwise it is supposed to be in `~/.codex/auth.json`
write a python script, `tools\check_dev_pace.py`, that checks the my current codex usage and report it against the limits.
if it fails, make it fail without an error return code, if used in a part of another script, it appears to succeed
add it as an end build script to `platformio.ini`

agent_reply: - Added [tools/check_dev_pace.py](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/tools/check_dev_pace.py:1), a stdlib-only checker that reads Codex auth, requests Codex rate-limit headers, reports 5-hour/7-day usage percent, remaining percent, reset time, and exits `0` even on failures.
- Kept both touched files normalized to CRLF.
- Missing-auth failure path: printed a skip message and returned `exit=0`
I used the repo you pointed at for the auth/header approach: https://github.com/Maol-1997/codex-stats
Note: I did not run the live Codex request with your real auth during validation, to avoid spending a service request. Git also reports `src/version.c` modified; I did not touch that file.

user_prompt: Codex usage check skipped: no Codex rate-limit headers were returned
agent_reply: Fixed. The 400 was from the probe request using a stale hard-coded model. [tools/check_dev_pace.py](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/tools/check_dev_pace.py:1) now reads `model = "..."` from your `.codex/config.toml` and falls back to `gpt-5-codex` only if no config model is found.
Also fixed a sneaky bug: `--verbose` was accidentally using `store_false`, so verbose logging was on by default. It now behaves normally.
I ran a live check after the fix and it succeeded:

I also kept the added verbose diagnostics, but response bodies now print only for HTTP errors so successful runs are not absurdly chatty. Syntax check passed, failure path still exits `0`, and the file is CRLF-normalized.

Fixed. The 400 was from the probe request using a stale hard-coded model. [tools/check_dev_pace.py](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/tools/check_dev_pace.py:1) now reads `model = "..."` from your `.codex/config.toml` and falls back to `gpt-5-codex` only if no config model is found.
Also fixed a sneaky bug: `--verbose` was accidentally using `store_false`, so verbose logging was on by default. It now behaves normally.
I ran a live check after the fix and it succeeded:

I also kept the added verbose diagnostics, but response bodies now print only for HTTP errors so successful runs are not absurdly chatty. Syntax check passed, failure path still exits `0`, and the file is CRLF-normalized.

user_prompt: if any timeouts are available to be set, try to make them short, like 5 seconds each max
agent_reply: Done. [tools/check_dev_pace.py](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/tools/check_dev_pace.py:21) now defaults to a 5-second network timeout and caps `--timeout` at 5 seconds.
still uses only `5` seconds internally. Verified syntax, help text, clamp behavior, and CRLF line endings.

user_prompt: Inside the `ScrollView` for Wi-Fi, the user can click on a `ScrollItem` representing a Wi-Fi router or initiate a scan-and-connect. This should lead to `ConnWaitingView`.
If the user cancels, the user should return to the `ScrollView` for Wi-Fi.
If the connection succeeds. There are two scenarios: at least one cloud upload destination is available, or none are available.

Create a `WifiStaModeView` much like `WifiApModeView`, borrow the same rough layout. There does not need to be a security icon. The SSID can always be shown in the same place and fonts and colour. The password is never shown. There does not need to be a show/hide button. The text should show “assigned IP:” and “gateway IP:”
The function where statistics are shown and cycled is kept.

If no cloud destinations are available, then `WifiStaModeView`  only shows the text about how you need to reset or power off to stop. Otherwise, there is a button `FlyViewItem` using `sprite_canceldoor_50` to dismiss the dialog to go back to the `ScrollView`, put this in the bottom right corner and tie it to `BtnC`.
If there is at least one cloud upload destination, then the `ScrollView` for cloud items will have an clickable item for just “information”, clicking that one leads to `WifiStaModeView`
Exiting out of the `ScrollView` for cloud items causes the microcontroller to reset.
agent_reply: Added [WifiStaModeView.h](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/WifiStaModeView.h>) and [WifiStaModeView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/WifiStaModeView.cpp>): it shows STA mode, SSID, assigned IP, gateway IP, and the same cycling web stats style as AP mode. When cloud destinations exist, it shows the `sprite_canceldoor_50` dismiss button on BtnC/bottom-right back to the cloud `ScrollView`; otherwise it shows the reset/power-off stop hint.
- Wi-Fi station click and scan-and-connect now go to `ConnWaitingView`.
- Cancel disconnects Wi-Fi and returns to the Wi-Fi `ScrollView`.
- Successful station connect starts the web server, then shows cloud `ScrollView` if destinations exist, otherwise `WifiStaModeView`.
- Cloud `ScrollView` has an `Information` item that opens `WifiStaModeView`.
- Exiting the cloud `ScrollView` resets the device.

Verification: `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passes, and `git diff --check` is clean. I restored the build-generated `src/version.c` change; there is an unrelated existing change in `tools/check_dev_pace.py` that I left alone.

user_prompt: make it so that, even if Wi-Fi connection has failed or cancelled, clicking the back button inside the ScrollView for Wi-Fi items will cause the microcontroller to reset. Have some sort of Wifi-has-started indication from WifiManager to do this
agent_reply: Done. I added a sticky `WifiManager::wifiHasStarted()` flag in [WifiManager.h](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.h>) / [WifiManager.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp>) that flips true once STA connect, STA scan, or AP mode actually starts. It is intentionally not cleared by `disconnect()`, so cancel/fail still counts until reset.

Then [ScrollView](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/ScrollView/ScrollView.h>) exposes `isWifiContext()`, and [main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp>) now resets when the ScrollView back button is pressed from Wi-Fi context after `wifiHasStarted()` is true.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`; build passed. Existing dirty sprite/check_dev_pace/version files were already in the worktree, and I left them alone.
